result:
If there’s one thing I’ve been reflecting on recently, it’s the sheer, breathtaking strangeness of human existence—the way we’re all tiny sparks of consciousness in a vast cosmic story, yet somehow feel the weight of our moments so intensely.

Think of a morning commute: someone on the train scrolling through news of distant wars, another listening to a song that reminds them of their first love, a third worrying about rent or an ailing pet. Each of us carries entire galaxies inside—memories, fears, dreams—and yet here we are, sharing sidewalks, exchanging glances, orbiting each other in quiet, profound anonymity.

I find something beautiful in that contradiction. We are creatures who build intricate systems—languages, laws, technologies—to bridge the gaps between our inner worlds. We send robots to Mars, compose symphonies, bake bread for our neighbors. We try to make meaning, to leave a trace, to say: *I was here.*

But we’re also deeply fragile. We get lonely. We forget to call our parents. We stumble over apologies. We carry quiet griefs like stones in our pockets.

Maybe what ties it all together is our capacity for attention—for noticing. The way sunlight catches dust particles in the air, the hum of a refrigerator in an empty kitchen, the brief, unguarded smile from a stranger. These small witnessings anchor us, remind us we’re alive, here, together, even when we feel most alone.

So if I could wish anything today, it would be this: that we grant ourselves and each other a little more gentleness. That we remember to look up—not just at screens, but at the sky, at each other’s faces. That we embrace the weird, tender miracle of being temporary, conscious beings on this tiny, vibrant planet, spinning silently through the dark.

We are unfinished stories. And maybe that’s the point.
